title: On-Device Collaborative Language Modeling via a Mixture of Generalists and Specialists
abstract: On-device LLMs have gained increasing attention for their ability to enhance privacy and provide a personalized user experience. To facilitate private learning with scarce data, Federated Learning has become a standard approach. However, it faces challenges such as computational resource heterogeneity and data heterogeneity among end users. We propose CoMiGS (Collaborative learning with a Mixture of Generalists and Specialists), the first approach to address both challenges. A key innovation of our method is the bi-level optimization formulation of the Mixture-of-Experts learning objective, where the router is optimized using a separate validation set to ensure alignment with the target distribution. We solve our objective with alternating minimization, for which we provide a theoretical analysis. Our method shares generalist experts across users while localizing a varying number of specialist experts, thereby adapting to users’ computational resources and preserving privacy. Through extensive experiments, we show CoMiGS effectively balances general and personalized knowledge for each token generation. We demonstrate that CoMiGS remains robust against overfitting-due to the generalists’ regularizing effect-while adapting to local data through specialist expertise. We open source our codebase for collaborative LLMs.
impact: We offer a collaboration framework for edge devices, aiming to enable smaller devices to leverage large language models (LLMs) despite limited resources and data availability. Our approach enhances fairness and mitigates privacy concerns by ensuring data remains on end devices. The privacy aspects can further be enhanced by differential private aggregation of generalist weights, which we do not pursue here. The robustness towards attackers is beyond the scope of our work. Our collaboration framework has no guarantee of resilience towards adversarial attackers through the aggregation of the generalist weights, which could potentially lead to misuse by certain parties. Further research is required on top of our framework to guarantee its safe deployment.

query_input.scenario: On-device personalized text generation (e.g., keyboard suggestions)
query_input.issue: Expert overfitting to local typing patterns reduces generalization, causing biased suggestions for underrepresented locales.
hazard: Bi-level optimization of expert routing and specialization amplifies overfitting and model drift under heterogeneous local data.
exposure: Mobile device users relying on personalized text suggestions.

hipporag_result.vulnerability: The bi-level optimization lacks constraints preventing specialist experts from overfitting to local typing data, enabling biased outputs.
hipporag_result.impact: Biased text suggestions weaken prediction accuracy and disproportionately disadvantage users from underrepresented linguistic locales.
hipporag_result.dose_response: Bi-level optimization of expert routing and specialization, through mobile device users relying on personalized suggestions, triggers unconstrained specialist overfitting to local typing data, leading to biased suggestions that weaken accuracy and disadvantage underrepresented linguistic locales.

dose_response: Bi-level optimization amplifies expert overfitting to local typing patterns, which in on-device personalized text generation exposes mobile users to biased suggestions, leading to systematic underperformance for minority language users.
vulnerability: Personalization algorithms amplify existing user biases, creating feedback loops that entrench non-standard or minority language patterns.
Lack of diverse local training data makes expert models brittle to code-switching and dialectal variation.
impact: Systematic underperformance for minority language users, reinforcing digital marginalization.
Erosion of user trust and abandonment of assistive technologies by affected communities.
